schools.summary: Gill: the things which become sound doctrine are a good life and conversation, the various duties incumbent on professors of religion according to their different station, age, and sex, which are observed in some following verses; Paul charging Titus to speak the things which become sound doctrine — the call to teach the practical duties that adorn the gospel (Paul, having exposed the false teachers, turning to Titus's own ministry with a 'but' of contrast: in opposition to the empty talkers, 'speak thou the things which become sound doctrine,' teach not idle fables but the wholesome truths and the holy duties that fittingly accompany and adorn the sound doctrine of the gospel, so Titus's preaching is to join right doctrine to right living, setting out the duties suited to each class of believer).

themes: but speak thou
the things which become sound doctrine
teaching the duties that adorn the gospel
right doctrine joined to right living

schools.summary: Chrysostom: there are some failings which age has that youth has not — some it has in common with youth, but in addition it has a slowness, a timidity, a forgetfulness, an insensibility, and an irritability — and for this reason Paul exhorts old men concerning these matters, 'to be vigilant,' for there are many things which at this period make them captious and hard to please; Paul directing that the aged men be sober, grave, temperate, and sound in faith, charity, and patience — the graces fitting for old age (Paul beginning with 'the aged men,' prescribing the virtues that should adorn their years: 'sober,' vigilant and self-restrained, watchful against the failings of age, 'grave,' dignified and serious in demeanour, 'temperate,' discreet and moderate, and, in the chief Christian graces, 'sound,' sound 'in faith,' firm and unshaken in believing, 'in charity,' in love, and 'in patience,' in steadfast endurance, so the old men of the church are to be examples of a ripe, settled, and healthy godliness in the three abiding graces).

themes: that the aged men be sober, grave, temperate
sound in faith, in charity, in patience
the graces fitting for old age
examples of a ripe, settled godliness

schools.summary: Gill: 'the aged women' design not persons in office (ancient widows who had some care of the poor, or presbyteresses, the wives of presbyters or elders, as distinct), but elderly women generally, who are to be spoken to about the things which become their profession and what is right for them to be and do; Paul directing that the aged women be in behaviour as becometh holiness, not false accusers, not given to much wine, teachers of good things — the holy conduct fitting for older women (Paul, 'likewise,' prescribing for the aged women the graces suited to them: that they be 'in behaviour as becometh holiness,' their whole demeanour reverent and seemly as befits a holy calling, 'not false accusers,' not slanderers or talebearers, 'not given to much wine,' not slaves to drink, and 'teachers of good things,' so that, rather than gossiping or tippling, the older women are to be reverent in bearing and to use their experience in teaching what is good, especially to the younger women).

themes: the aged women likewise, that they be in behaviour as becometh holiness
not false accusers, not given to much wine
teachers of good things
reverent older women using their experience well

schools.summary: Gill: the aged women are to teach the young women to be sober — or to be chaste, modest, and temperate, or to be wise and prudent in their conduct toward their husbands and in the management of family affairs, the older women having had a large experience of these things before them; Paul directing that the older women teach the young women to be sober, to love their husbands and their children — the older women's role in training the young in home and family (Paul giving the great use of the older women's teaching of good things: 'that they may teach the young women,' instructing them by word and example in their proper duties, beginning with the home: 'to be sober,' discreet and self-controlled, 'to love their husbands,' to be affectionate and faithful wives, and 'to love their children,' to be tender, devoted mothers, so the experienced women are to train the younger in the foundational graces of a godly wife and mother).

themes: that they may teach the young women to be sober
to love their husbands
to love their children
training the young in the graces of wife and mother

schools.summary: Barnes: 'discreet' is the same word rendered 'temperate' in v.2 and explained in v.4, here applied to the young women, and 'chaste' means pure in heart and life; Gill: to be discreet is to be temperate in eating and drinking, sober in body and mind, wise and prudent in their whole conduct at home and abroad; Paul directing that the young women be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good, obedient to their husbands, that the word of God be not blasphemed — the household virtues that guard the gospel's good name (Paul completing the list of graces the young women are to learn: 'discreet,' sober-minded and self-controlled, 'chaste,' pure in heart and body, 'keepers at home,' diligent in the care and management of their households, 'good,' kind and benevolent, and 'obedient to their own husbands,' in due subjection to their own husbands, and naming the motive that crowns all, 'that the word of God be not blasphemed,' lest the gospel be reproached through the ill conduct of Christian wives).

themes: to be discreet, chaste, keepers at home, good
obedient to their own husbands
that the word of God be not blasphemed
household virtues guarding the gospel's name

schools.summary: Gill: this — to exhort the young men to be sober minded, temperate, chaste, modest, moderate, wise, and prudent in all things — is said to Titus as being his province to instruct and exhort the young men, as it was proper and convenient for aged women to teach the young women how they should behave; Paul directing that the young men likewise be exhorted to be sober minded — the one great grace pressed on the young, self-control (Paul, turning to the young men, summing their duty in a single comprehensive word: 'exhort' them 'to be sober minded,' that is to be self-controlled, temperate, discreet, and prudent in all things, for the chief danger and the chief need of youth is the governing of its passions and appetites, and so the one grace of a sound, sober mind is pressed upon them, this being Titus's own charge to deliver).

themes: young men likewise exhort
to be sober minded
the one grace of self-control pressed on youth
governing the passions of youth

schools.summary: Gill: it was not enough for Titus, and so neither for any other gospel minister, to deliver out sound doctrine and exhort persons of different ages and sexes to the things which become it, but he should through the whole of his conversation be a pattern of every good work; Paul charging Titus to show himself in all things a pattern of good works, and in his doctrine to show uncorruptness, gravity, and sincerity — the minister's own life and teaching as a model (Paul pressing Titus to back his teaching with his own example: 'in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works,' a living model that others may copy, his life adorning what he preaches, and that his teaching itself be marked by 'uncorruptness,' purity free from error and corrupt motive, 'gravity,' a seriousness and dignity befitting holy things, and 'sincerity,' honesty and integrity with no guile, so the minister is to preach as much by his life and the character of his teaching as by his words).

themes: in all things shewing thyself a pattern of good works
in doctrine shewing uncorruptness, gravity, sincerity
the minister's own life and teaching as a model
preaching by life as by words

schools.summary: Gill: in the public ministry the wholesome words of our Lord Jesus should be used, and the doctrines of the gospel expressed as near as can be in the words which the Holy Ghost teaches, not in the enticing words of man's wisdom; such speech or language should be chosen that is plain and sound; Paul charging that Titus use sound speech that cannot be condemned, so that the opponent may be ashamed, having no evil to say — the unimpeachable speech that puts adversaries to shame (Paul completing the charge to Titus's teaching with a word about his speech: it must be 'sound speech, that cannot be condemned,' wholesome, true, and so blameless that no just fault can be found in it, and to a clear end, 'that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed, having no evil thing to say of you,' that the gospel's adversaries, finding nothing to censure in his doctrine or life, might be put to silence and shame, so a blameless ministry is itself a defence against the enemies of the truth).

themes: sound speech, that cannot be condemned
that he that is of the contrary part may be ashamed
having no evil thing to say of you
a blameless ministry a defence against the enemy

schools.summary: Gill: servants are to be obedient to their own masters, and not others, whether they be believers or unbelievers, gentle or froward — all their lawful commands ought to be obeyed — and to please them well in all things, not only obeying and serving them and doing what they order but seeking and endeavouring to do it in such a manner as to give satisfaction; Paul charging that servants be obedient to their own masters, please them well in all things, and not answer again — the obedience and good temper required of Christian servants (Paul, turning to servants, a large class in the early church, bidding Titus exhort them to a threefold duty: 'to be obedient unto their own masters,' yielding due submission in all lawful things, 'to please them well in all things,' seeking by diligent willing service to give satisfaction, and 'not answering again,' not contradicting, talking back, or replying with insolence, so the Christian servant is to be marked by ready obedience and a meek, respectful temper).

themes: exhort servants to be obedient unto their own masters
to please them well in all things
not answering again
ready obedience joined to a meek temper

schools.summary: Gill: 'not purloining' is not stealing or embezzling their master's substance, not taking away and making use of what is their property, not keeping back part of money or goods committed to their trust (the word used in the case of Ananias and Sapphira); Paul charging that servants not purloin but show all good fidelity, that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour — the faithful honesty of servants that beautifies the gospel (Paul completing the servant's duty: 'not purloining,' not pilfering or secretly stealing from the master nor embezzling what is entrusted, 'but shewing all good fidelity,' proving themselves perfectly trustworthy in all that is committed to them, and crowning it with the highest motive, 'that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour in all things,' that even the humblest believing slave, by honest and faithful service, might make the gospel beautiful and commend it to all, so lowly fidelity is itself an ornament to the doctrine of Christ).

themes: not purloining, but shewing all good fidelity
that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Saviour
the faithful honesty of servants
lowly fidelity an ornament to the gospel

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having demanded from servants so great virtue (for it is great virtue to adorn the doctrine of our God and Saviour in all things) and charged them to give no occasion of offence to their masters even in common matters, Paul adds the just cause why servants should be such: 'for the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared'; Paul declaring that the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men — the saving grace of God now manifest as the ground of all holy living (Paul giving the deep reason for all the foregoing duties: it is 'the grace of God that bringeth salvation,' the free saving favour of God embodied and made visible in the gospel of Christ, which 'hath appeared,' shone forth like a sunrise upon the world, 'to all men,' to men of every sort, Jew and Gentile, master and slave, not to one nation only, so the very purpose and tendency of this manifested grace is to teach men to live holy lives, grace being not only the ground of salvation but the great school of godliness).

themes: for the grace of God that bringeth salvation
hath appeared to all men
the saving grace of God now manifest
grace the ground and school of holy living

schools.summary: Gill: grace teaches not all men to whom the gospel appears in its outward ministry (for many externally receive and profess it who are never influentially taught by it to deny sin or love holiness, having a form of godliness but denying its power), but those whom it effectually teaches it leads to deny sin and love a holy life; Paul declaring that grace teaches us, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present world — the threefold holy life that the grace of God trains us to live (Paul showing the very lesson the appeared grace teaches its true pupils, a twofold work of renunciation and pursuit: 'denying ungodliness and worldly lusts,' renouncing all impiety toward God and all the sinful cravings of the world, and, positively, that 'we should live soberly,' in self-control toward ourselves, 'righteously,' in justice toward others, and 'godly,' in piety toward God, and that 'in this present world,' here and now amid its temptations, so grace is no licence to sin but the very teacher of a complete and holy life).

debates.question: What does the grace of God teach its true pupils?

sides.position: Not licence to sin but a complete holy life — the denial of ungodliness and worldly lusts, and a sober, righteous, godly walk toward self, neighbour, and God; grace is the very school of godliness

themes: teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts
we should live soberly, righteously, and godly
in this present world
grace the school of a threefold holy life

schools.summary: Gill: 'that blessed hope' is not the grace of hope (which the saints already have implanted in regeneration and cannot be said to look for), but rather Christ himself, the object and ground of hope, who is our hope, and the glory and happiness hoped for, to be revealed at his appearing; Paul declaring that we are looking for that blessed hope and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ — the forward-looking hope fixed on Christ's glorious appearing and his deity (Paul adding the upward and forward look that accompanies the holy life: while living godly now, the believer 'looking for that blessed hope,' waiting in joyful expectation for the blessed object of his hope, 'and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ'; this striking phrase, by its very construction, naming Jesus Christ as 'the great God and our Saviour,' a clear witness to the full deity of Christ, whose appearing in glory is the believer's blessed hope).

debates.question: Whom does 'the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ' name?

sides.position: Jesus Christ himself — the phrase by its construction names Christ as 'the great God and our Saviour,' a clear witness to his full deity, whose glorious appearing is the believer's blessed hope

themes: looking for that blessed hope
and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ
the blessed hope of Christ's glorious appearing
a clear witness to the full deity of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Christ gave not another, nor another's, but himself; not merely his own things but his own self; not the world and its riches, not gold and silver, not the cattle on a thousand hills for sacrifice, not men nor angels, but himself, all that belonged to him, as the price of redemption; Paul declaring that Christ gave himself for us to redeem us from all iniquity and purify a peculiar people zealous of good works — the redeeming, purifying self-sacrifice of Christ and its purpose (Paul grounding the blessed hope in the great work of the Saviour who is its object: he 'gave himself for us,' his very self in his atoning death as the ransom-price, and that with a twofold purpose, 'that he might redeem us from all iniquity,' deliver us by his blood from the guilt and dominion of all sin, and 'purify unto himself a peculiar people,' cleanse for himself a people of his own special possession, 'zealous of good works,' so Christ died not only to forgive but to form a holy, eager, good-working people for his own).

themes: who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all iniquity
purify unto himself a peculiar people
zealous of good works
Christ dying to forgive and to form a holy people

schools.summary: Gill: 'these things' are sound doctrine — the doctrine of grace, the doctrines of salvation and redemption by Christ, of peace, pardon, and cleansing by his blood — which Titus is to speak out clearly, plainly, publicly, boldly, and faithfully, and the things which become sound doctrine, the duties of religion suitable to every age and station; Paul charging Titus to speak, exhort, and rebuke these things with all authority, letting no man despise him — the authority with which the minister is to teach and the respect he is to command (Paul closing the chapter by charging Titus to deliver all the foregoing, the sound doctrine and holy duties, with full ministerial authority: 'these things speak,' declare them, 'and exhort,' urge them upon the people, 'and rebuke,' reprove the disobedient, 'with all authority,' in the full commission of Christ's minister, and adding 'let no man despise thee,' so to bear himself in life and teaching that none can rightly slight his ministry, so the minister is to teach with boldness and maintain the dignity of his office).

themes: these things speak, and exhort, and rebuke with all authority
let no man despise thee
the authority of the minister's teaching
teaching with boldness, maintaining the office's dignity
